blog_articles.title: How Often to Feed a 10-Week-Old Puppy
blog_articles.summary: Learn how many meals per day a 10-week-old puppy needs, how much to feed at each meal, and how to adjust as they grow.
blog_articles.content:
At 10 weeks old, most puppies do best on three to four small meals per day. Their stomachs are still developing, and frequent meals help keep energy levels stable while avoiding overfilling the digestive system.

**Typical schedule for a 10-week-old puppy**
- Morning: Breakfast around 7–8 a.m.
- Midday: Lunch around 12–1 p.m.
- Late afternoon: Dinner around 5–6 p.m.
- Optional small evening snack if recommended by your veterinarian.

Always start with the feeding guidelines on your puppy food’s package, then adjust based on your puppy’s body condition and activity level. Puppies should have a visible waist when viewed from above and a slight tummy tuck when viewed from the side.

Introduce new foods gradually over 5–7 days to prevent digestive upset. Combine your puppy’s current food with the new one, slowly increasing the amount of the new food while decreasing the old.

blog_articles.title: Puppy Feeding Schedule by Age: Printable Chart
blog_articles.summary: A simple chart that shows how many meals per day and how much to feed puppies from 8 weeks to 12 months.
blog_articles.content:
Puppies grow quickly in their first year, and their feeding needs change just as fast. Use this age-based schedule as a starting point, then fine-tune based on your veterinarian’s advice.

**General guidelines**
- 8–12 weeks: 3–4 meals per day
- 3–6 months: 3 meals per day
- 6–12 months: 2 meals per day

Within each age band, check your food’s calorie content and use our Feeding Calculator to personalize daily portions. Remember to account for treats: they should make up no more than 10% of daily calories.

blog_articles.title: How Much Should a 25 kg Adult Dog Eat Per Day?
blog_articles.summary: Learn how to estimate daily calories and portions for a moderately active 25 kg adult dog.
blog_articles.content:
A moderately active 25 kg adult dog typically needs around 1,200–1,400 kcal per day. However, the exact amount depends on age, body condition, and the calorie density of the food you choose.

Step 1: Check the calorie content (kcal/kg or kcal/cup) on your dog food package.
Step 2: Use our online Feeding Calculator to enter your dog’s weight, activity level, and chosen recipe.
Step 3: Divide the recommended daily amount into 2 meals per day to support healthy digestion and consistent energy.

Reassess your dog’s body condition every 2–4 weeks and adjust portions by 5–10% as needed.

blog_articles.title: Understanding Grain-Free Dog Food: When Is It Right for Your Dog?
blog_articles.summary: Explore what grain-free means, when it may be helpful, and how to choose a complete and balanced grain-free formula.
blog_articles.content:
Grain-free dog foods replace traditional grains like rice, corn, and barley with alternative sources of carbohydrates such as sweet potatoes or peas. For many dogs, grains are well tolerated and provide important nutrients. Grain-free formulas can be helpful for dogs with certain suspected grain sensitivities, but they are not automatically healthier.

When choosing a grain-free food, look for:
- A named animal protein (such as salmon or chicken) as the first ingredient.
- An AAFCO nutritional adequacy statement for your dog’s life stage.
- Transparent feeding guidelines and clear ingredient lists.

If you are considering switching to a grain-free formula, talk with your veterinarian and transition slowly over 7–10 days to avoid digestive upset.

blog_articles.title: Weight Control for Indoor Cats: A Practical Feeding Guide
blog_articles.summary: How to feed your indoor cat for a healthy weight, including tips on portioning dry and wet foods.
blog_articles.content:
Indoor cats are less active than outdoor cats, which means they burn fewer calories. To help your cat maintain a healthy weight:

- Choose a formula labeled for indoor or weight management.
- Use a kitchen scale or measuring cup to portion food accurately.
- Combine wet and dry foods to support hydration while controlling calories.
- Offer multiple short play sessions each day to increase activity.

Our Indoor Weight Control recipes are formulated with moderate fat levels and tailored fiber to help your cat feel satisfied between meals.

blog_articles.title: Wet vs. Dry Cat Food: Which Is Better for Your Cat?
blog_articles.summary: Compare the benefits of wet and dry cat food and learn how to create a mixed feeding plan.
blog_articles.content:
Both wet and dry cat foods can be complete and balanced. Dry foods are convenient, support dental crunching, and are easy to store, while wet foods boost moisture intake and can be more enticing to picky eaters.

Many veterinarians recommend a mixed feeding approach: a portion of dry food for grazing and a portion of wet food for hydration and variety. Our Feeding Plan tool helps you calculate the right combination based on your cat’s weight and activity level.

blog_articles.title: Creating a Mixed Feeding Plan for Indoor Hairball Control Cats
blog_articles.summary: How to combine Indoor Hairball dry and wet foods into a balanced daily plan for adult indoor cats.
blog_articles.content:
For cats prone to hairballs, nutrition and hydration both matter. Our Indoor Hairball line includes coordinating dry and wet recipes designed to work together.

To build a mixed plan:
1. Choose one Indoor Hairball dry food and one Indoor Hairball wet food.
2. Enter your cat’s weight into the Feeding Plan tool and select “mixed feeding: dry + wet.”
3. Follow the recommended split between dry and wet food, adjusting by 5–10% based on your cat’s body condition.

Always transition gradually when changing your cat’s diet to minimize digestive upset.

blog_articles.title: Nutrition Tips for Senior Large-Breed Dogs with Joint Concerns
blog_articles.summary: Key nutrients and feeding strategies to support mobility in senior large-breed dogs.
blog_articles.content:
As large-breed dogs age, joint comfort becomes a top priority. Look for recipes formulated for senior or mobility support that include glucosamine, chondroitin, and omega-3 fatty acids from sources like fish oil.

Keep your dog at a lean body condition, as excess weight puts additional strain on joints. Use our Product Finder quiz to identify formulas tailored for senior large-breed dogs with joint concerns, and talk to your veterinarian about the right feeding plan.

blog_articles.title: Choosing Puppy Treats for Training Without Overfeeding
blog_articles.summary: How to pick the right treats for puppies and keep treat calories within healthy limits.
blog_articles.content:
Training sessions often involve lots of treats, especially for puppies who are learning everything for the first time. To avoid overfeeding:

- Choose small, soft treats specifically labeled for puppies.
- Break larger treats into tiny pieces.
- Keep treat calories under 10% of your puppy’s daily intake.
- Balance training sessions with appropriate meal portions.

Our puppy treats are designed to be low in calories per piece and easy to chew, making them ideal for frequent rewards.

blog_articles.title: Our Brand Story: Sustainable Ingredients, Happier Pets
blog_articles.summary: Discover how we source ingredients and craft recipes that are good for pets and the planet.
blog_articles.content:
From day one, our mission has been to create nutritionally sound recipes using responsibly sourced ingredients. We partner with farms and fisheries that prioritize animal welfare and environmental stewardship.

Our kitchens use small-batch cooking, and every recipe is tested for quality and safety before it reaches your pet’s bowl. We also continue to reduce packaging waste and improve recyclability with each new product line.

blog_articles.title: Stay Informed: Dog and Cat Nutrition Tips in Your Inbox
blog_articles.summary: Why signing up for our email tips can help you stay on top of your pet’s nutritional needs.
blog_articles.content:
Our newsletters deliver practical feeding advice, new product updates, and seasonal tips straight to your inbox. Select interests like dog nutrition, puppy training, or indoor cat care so we can tailor the content you receive.

You can unsubscribe at any time, and we’ll never share your email address with third parties.

blog_articles.title: How Often Should I Feed My Dog?
blog_articles.summary: Guidelines on meal frequency for puppies, adults, and senior dogs.
blog_articles.content:
Most adult dogs thrive on two meals per day: one in the morning and one in the evening. Puppies need three to four meals spaced evenly throughout the day, while some seniors do better on three smaller meals.

Consider your dog’s lifestyle, health status, and any veterinary recommendations when setting a schedule. Consistency helps support digestion and can make house training easier for puppies.

help_articles.title: How to Switch Your Dog to a New Food
help_articles.content:
A gradual transition helps prevent digestive upset when switching your dog to a new food. We recommend transitioning over 7–10 days.

**7-day transition example**
- Days 1–2: 75% current food, 25% new food
- Days 3–4: 50% current food, 50% new food
- Days 5–6: 25% current food, 75% new food
- Day 7: 100% new food

Monitor your dog’s stool quality, appetite, and energy levels during the transition. If you notice loose stool or vomiting, slow the transition by repeating the previous step for a few more days. Always keep fresh water available, and consult your veterinarian if symptoms persist.

You can apply this same transition schedule when switching between any of our formulas, whether grain-free, limited ingredient, or life-stage-specific recipes.

help_articles.title: How the Feeding Calculator Works
help_articles.content:
Our Feeding Calculator uses your pet’s weight, life stage, activity level, and selected recipe to estimate a daily feeding amount. The tool is designed as a starting point and does not replace veterinary advice.

To use the calculator:
1. Select your pet type (dog or cat).
2. Choose a specific recipe from the dropdown.
3. Enter your pet’s weight and select the correct unit (kg or lb).
4. Choose an activity level.
5. Click "Calculate" to see the recommended daily amount.

You can save the recommendation to a pet profile to track changes over time.

help_articles.title: How Much Should My Dog Eat?
help_articles.content:
The right portion for your dog depends on age, ideal body weight, activity level, and the calorie density of the food. Start with the feeding guide printed on your bag or can, then adjust based on your dog’s body condition.

Signs your dog may be eating too much include rapid weight gain, loss of a visible waist, and difficulty feeling ribs. If your dog seems hungry on the recommended portion but is at a healthy weight, consider splitting the daily amount into more frequent meals or adding low-calorie vegetables as snacks.

help_articles.title: Finding Our Products in Local Stores
help_articles.content:
Use our Store Locator to find retailers near you that carry our products. Enter your ZIP code and select a search radius. You can use filters such as "carries dry food" to narrow the results.

Some stores may carry only a selection of our products. For specific formulas, use the product search filter within the locator (where available) and enter the product name, such as "Small Breed Adult Chicken." Call the store ahead of your visit to confirm current stock.

help_articles.title: Indoor Cat Weight Control FAQ
help_articles.content:
Indoor cats often need fewer calories than outdoor cats but still benefit from mentally stimulating mealtimes. Our Indoor Weight Control and Indoor Hairball recipes are formulated with tailored calories and fibers.

Common questions include how to transition to a weight-control food, how to combine dry and wet recipes, and how quickly you should expect weight changes. In general, aim for gradual weight loss of 1–2% of body weight per week. Always consult your veterinarian before starting a weight loss plan.

help_articles.title: Managing Dog Food Sensitivities and Allergies
help_articles.content:
If your dog experiences recurring itchiness, ear infections, or digestive upset, talk to your veterinarian about potential food sensitivities. Common triggers include specific proteins like chicken or beef.

Our limited-ingredient and grain-free lines offer options that avoid certain proteins and grains. Always transition slowly to a new diet and track symptoms in a journal. Do not attempt an elimination diet without veterinary guidance.

help_articles.title: Orders and Payments FAQ
help_articles.content:
This article covers common questions about placing orders, accepted payment methods, billing, and shipping timelines. We accept major credit cards and popular digital wallets. Orders usually ship within 1–2 business days.

For order-specific questions, please contact our support team with your order number.

help_articles.title: How to Contact Customer Support
help_articles.content:
You can reach our support team through the Contact Us form on our website, by email, or by phone. For nutrition-related questions, select the topic "Feeding & Portions" or "Nutrition" in the contact form to reach our specialists.

We typically respond to messages within 1–2 business days. For urgent concerns about your pet’s health, please contact your veterinarian immediately.
